Never has the supplier missed three consecutive deadlines before.
Not only did they fail to submit the schedule, but they also sent an incomplete revision.
Hardly had we begun the factory acceptance test when a critical non-conformance was identified.
Under no circumstances will we accept a fourth revision of the schedule.

Thanks for making the time, Katrin. I will be direct. The revised baseline schedule is three weeks overdue, and it would be remiss of us not to flag that this is the third missed milestone on the package.
I understand. As I explained in writing, the strike at the port was outside our control. We consider this a force majeure event.

I have read Clause 14 carefully. The strike was announced six weeks in advance, so our legal team does not believe force majeure applies. Not only did the schedule slip, but no new date has been confirmed in writing either.
That is a strong position, João. Are you telling me you are going to invoke the penalty clause?
Under no circumstances do I want to start there. But I have to be honest with you: if the fourth deadline is missed, I am afraid we are left with no alternative but to invoke it.
And what would you like from us instead?

Let me put forward a proposal. You give me a recovery plan by Friday, with a date we can both defend, and we treat the minutes of this call as a binding agreement. If we disagree later, we go to mediation before anyone mentions arbitration.
I would rather bite the bullet now than end up in arbitration next year. You will have the plan on Friday, and I will confirm the date in writing.
It would be remiss of us not to flag that this is the third missed milestone.
Never has this package been three weeks behind the baseline schedule.
Not only did the schedule slip, but no new date has been confirmed in writing.
I'm afraid we're left with no alternative but to invoke the penalty clause.
We would much rather reach a resolution through mediation than pursue a formal complaint.

João, it is Katrin. I owe you an answer before Friday, and I would rather leave it here than in an email. First, the recovery plan. It is with our planning team tonight, and you will have it on Friday, with a date my own director has already agreed to defend. Second, force majeure. I took your argument to our legal department, and they agree with yours. The strike was announced six weeks in advance, so we are withdrawing the claim. Never before have we withdrawn a force majeure claim on a project of this size, and I wanted you to hear that from me and not from a lawyer. Third, and this is the difficult part. The indemnity in Clause 9 is still open, and my finance director will push back on it. I am not going to pretend otherwise. What I can tell you is that I would rather put a proposal forward and bite the bullet on the cost than have the two of us in arbitration next year. Talk on Friday.

Before you send that escalation email, three things about contracts, because engineers get these wrong more often than lawyers do. First. A penalty clause is not a weapon, it is a deterrent. The moment you invoke it, you have told the supplier that the relationship is over and that the lawyers are in charge. Hardly had we invoked the clause on the Line 2 package when the supplier stopped answering the phone, and every small favor we relied on disappeared. Use it late, and use it once. Second. Never confuse mediation with arbitration. In mediation, a neutral third party helps the two of you reach your own agreement, and either side can walk away. In arbitration, the third party decides for you, and the decision is binding. One is a conversation. The other is a judgment. Third. Write everything down. A promise on a call is not a binding agreement. The minutes you send afterwards, if nobody objects to them, very often are. And one last word about force majeure. It means an event that nobody could plan around. A strike announced six weeks in advance is not force majeure. It is a schedule risk, and the liability for it is contractual.
